summary: Jeff Atwood summarizes Marcus Ranum's influential essay on the fundamental flaws in computer security thinking. The post highlights six common but misguided approaches: default permit policies, blacklist-based security, reactive patching, glorifying hackers, relying on user education, and rushing to adopt new solutions. Atwood emphasizes that Microsoft's shift to 'off by default' with Windows Server 2003 demonstrates how addressing even one of these issues can dramatically improve security. The post concludes with a reflection on client-side security, suggesting that until PCs require hardware modifications to run custom software (like game consoles), client security will remain fundamentally compromised.
key_points: Default permit ('on by default') policies were the primary reason Windows security was considered an oxymoron for years
Blacklists are fundamentally flawed as a primary defense mechanism and should only serve helper roles
Security must be designed from the inside out - patching cannot fix fundamentally bad security architecture
Relying on user education as a security measure ignores the reality that users are fallible by default
Client PCs are inherently untrustworthy until hardware-level restrictions prevent arbitrary code execution
money_quotes: The good news is that Microsoft's new policy of 'off by default' that kicked off with Windows Server 2003 is really working.
This is why blacklists are, and always will be, a bad idea.
Security starts from the inside, not the outside. No amount of patching will fix a fundamentally bad security design.
A security system that fails to assume users are fallible and weak by default is destined to fail spectacularly.
You can always recognize the pioneers from all the arrows in their backs.
I think it's a myth that you can protect yourself from the client PC anyway – the client is always suspect.
key_insight: Most security failures stem not from sophisticated attacks but from fundamentally flawed assumptions baked into system design from the start.
